nodes.label: Ambition over complacency
nodes.seed_framing: Schools settled into their current shape out of historical constraint, not because that shape is what learning needs. New technology should let us chase what was previously impossible, not optimize the compromise.

nodes.label: Augmentation, not efficiency
nodes.seed_framing:
The framing battle. "Efficiency" in education usually means doing the existing thing faster, which entrenches it. "Augmentation" means enabling kinds of learning that couldn't happen without the tool. Most edtech sits on the wrong side of this line.

**Steer (2026-05-27):** An interactive session surfaced an apparent synthesis — KSC's expertise-reversal concession plus Illich's skill-drill concession plus Papert's transitional-object case all seemed to support a two-phase framing: "AI builds the prior-knowledge floor (efficiency win) → ambitious inquiry becomes available later (augmentation)." **The thesis rejects this synthesis.** Reading Dewey ¶8 ("education is a process of living and not a preparation for future living") as instrumental — early years are floor-building for later…

nodes.label: Teacher agency
nodes.seed_framing: Scripted curricula and tools-as-replacement are eroding the conditions for great teaching. Any AI-in-education vision that further reduces teacher judgment is a category error.

nodes.label: Tech cycles in education
nodes.seed_framing: Every prior wave of technology in education (cinema, radio, television, programmed-instruction machines, classroom computers, MOOCs) was introduced with very similar optimistic rhetoric, and most failed to change what actually happens in schools. Cuban's historical record is the steelman the optimistic case has to clear: if the pattern is "hype, modest adoption, absorption into the existing grammar of schooling, disappointment," then the burden is on showing what is structurally different this time. Not whether AI is different in capability — *every* prior wave was different in capability — but whether the *adoption pathway* into ambitious learning is different. This thread holds the others honest.

nodes.label: Anthony S. Bryk 2010
nodes.title: Organizing Schools for Improvement: Lessons from Chicago (symposium presentation)
nodes.thesis_excerpt:
This is the *slide deck* from the January 2010 CCSR symposium accompanying the
book *Organizing Schools for Improvement: Lessons from Chicago* (University of
Chicago Press, 2010). It is not the book itself. It is a 5-page summary of the
study's headline findings: a 15-year longitudinal study of 100+ Chicago
elementary schools that identified **five "Essential Supports"** as
distinguishing schools that substantially improved in reading and math from
those that stagnated, and that quantified the relationship between each
support's strength and the likelihood of substantial improvement.

The…

nodes.label: Larry Cuban 2024
nodes.title: Will AI Transform Teaching and Learning?
nodes.thesis_excerpt:
Cuban's compact contemporary statement of his classic position, applied to AI.
Adapted from a five-member Google panel he sat on as the lone historian. The
argument has three moves: (1) every prior classroom technology of the last
century — radio, 16mm film, overhead projectors, instructional TV,
videocassettes, 1:1 laptops, interactive whiteboards — was introduced with
"revolutionary" and "transformational" rhetoric; (2) none of them produced
evidence of fundamental change in how teachers teach, student engagement, or
test scores; (3) the reason this happens is not that the technologies…

nodes.label: Larry Cuban 2025
nodes.title: How Some Teachers Use AI
nodes.thesis_excerpt:
A genre shift from Cuban 2024. Where the November 2024 post was Cuban
*predicting* — his historian's voice applying his Cuban-1986 frame to AI —
the March 2025 post is Cuban *curating*. He opens with a single declarative
sentence ("I have gathered comments from teachers who have used AI in daily
lessons and grading student work") and then steps out of the frame. The body
is teacher voices, lightly edited, drawn from press accounts and direct
quotes. Cuban offers no analysis, no concluding paragraph, no prediction.
That structure is the argument: the absorption Cuban 2024 predicted is…

nodes.label: John Dewey 1897
nodes.title: My Pedagogic Creed
nodes.thesis_excerpt:
A 73-numbered-belief credo, organized in five articles (What Education Is;
What the School Is; The Subject-Matter of Education; The Nature of Method;
The School and Social Progress). Dewey argues that education is a *social*
process — not a preparation for future life but a continuous reconstruction
of present experience — and that the teacher is a member of a community
selecting which influences should shape the child, not a transmitter of
predetermined content. School is "primarily a social institution," and any
education that does not occur through "forms of life... worth living for…

nodes.label: Cindy E. Hmelo-Silver 2007
nodes.title: Scaffolding and Achievement in Problem-Based and Inquiry Learning: A Response to Kirschner, Sweller, and Clark (2006)
nodes.thesis_excerpt:
The published rejoinder to Kirschner, Sweller & Clark (2006). HSDC argue that
KSC's case is built on two flaws. First, a *pedagogical* flaw: KSC conflate
problem-based learning (PBL) and inquiry learning (IL) with unguided
discovery learning, when in fact PBL and IL as actually practiced are
extensively scaffolded — through expert modeling, just-in-time direct
instruction, tool-imposed structure (Model-It's plan/build/test modes;
GenScope's linked representations; WISE's embedded hints; anchored
instruction's whiteboards), and curriculum-level constraints. The forms of
guidance present in…

nodes.label: Ivan Illich 1971
nodes.title: Deschooling Society
nodes.thesis_excerpt:
Illich's argument is more radical than the standard "schools could be better"
critique: schooling itself, as an institution, manufactures the dependencies
it claims to remedy. The "hidden curriculum" of school is the equation of
process with substance — students are trained to confuse "teaching with
learning, grade advancement with education, a diploma with competence."
Universal schooling, on this view, cannot be fixed by reform (better
teachers, better technology, more funding); it must be replaced with
"educational webs which heighten the opportunity for each one to transform
each moment…

"Many students, especially those who are poor, intuitively know what the
"We need research on the possible use of technology to create institutions

nodes.label: Katy Jordan 2014
nodes.title: Initial Trends in Enrolment and Completion of Massive Open Online Courses
nodes.thesis_excerpt:
A meta-aggregation of publicly available enrolment and completion data for
the early generation of MOOCs (Coursera, EdX, Udacity, courses through July
2013; n=91 courses with enrolment data, n=39 with completion data). Jordan
finds that the early headline numbers (the Stanford AI course, 160,000
enrolled / 20,000 completing) are atypical; the median MOOC enrolment is
~43,000 and the median completion rate is 6.5% of enrollees (9.8% of "active"
students, where active = at least one login / quiz / video). Completion rates
are *consistent* across time, university ranking, and total enrolment,…

"Six-figure enrolment statistics have generated a good deal of interest in
"Completion rates range from 0.9% to 36.1%, with a median value of 6.5%

nodes.label: Paul A. Kirschner 2006
nodes.title: Why Minimal Guidance During Instruction Does Not Work: An Analysis of the Failure of Constructivist, Discovery, Problem-Based, Experiential, and Inquiry-Based Teaching
nodes.thesis_excerpt:
A polemical synthesis of cognitive-load theory and a half-century of experimental
work, arguing that minimally guided instructional approaches (discovery,
problem-based, inquiry, experiential, and constructivist teaching) are
inconsistent with what is known about human cognitive architecture — in
particular, the severe capacity limits of working memory when processing novel
information — and that controlled studies "almost uniformly" favour direct
guided instruction for novice and intermediate learners. The authors name
Bruner (1961), Papert (1980), and Steffe & Gale (1995) as exemplars of…

nodes.label: Seymour Papert 1980
nodes.title: The Gears of My Childhood" (Foreword to *Mindstorms: Children, Computers, and Powerful Ideas*)
nodes.thesis_excerpt:
Papert tells the autobiographical story of falling in love with the
differential gear before age two — an object simultaneously sensorimotor
("you can be the gear") and abstract (groups, relative motion) that became
the model through which mathematics later "carried" itself into his mind.
From this, the central claim: powerful learning happens when a learner finds,
or is given, *transitional objects* that connect "body knowledge" to formal
structure, and the affective bond ("I fell in love with the gears") is not a
side effect but constitutive. The book's program — and the…

nodes.label: Marlene Scardamalia 1994
nodes.title: Computer Support for Knowledge-Building Communities
nodes.thesis_excerpt:
The foundational paper articulating *knowledge building* as a "third way" for
schooling — distinct from didactic instruction and from child-centered
discovery — and describing CSILE (Computer-Supported Intentional Learning
Environments) as the enabling technology. The argument has four moves: (1)
contemporary schools are *first-order environments* in which adaptation is
asymptotic; the alternative is the *second-order environment* of scientific
research disciplines, where each participant's adaptation changes the
environment for others, producing non-asymptotic learning. (2) The diagnosis
of…

nodes.label: John Sweller 2007
nodes.title: Why Minimally Guided Teaching Techniques Do Not Work: A Reply to Commentaries
nodes.thesis_excerpt:
The published sur-rejoinder by Sweller, Kirschner & Clark to the two
commentaries on their 2006 paper — Schmidt, Loyens, van Gog & Paas (2007)
and Hmelo-Silver, Duncan & Chinn (2007). The piece does *not* concede ground;
it reasserts and sharpens the original argument. Three moves matter for the
ledger:

(1) **The worked-example effect as the empirical anchor.** SKC argue that

solutions over problem-solving search for novice learners — is the

nodes.label: Audrey Watters 2018
nodes.title: Machine Teaching, Machine Learning, and the History of the Future of Public Education
nodes.thesis_excerpt:
Watters' two-assertion frame: (1) the role of corporations and philanthropists in
shaping education policy is *not* new — the ed-tech industry has been at this for
over a century, and the marketing rhetoric has *always* been the same: their
products will "augment what teachers can do. Augment, not replace, of course";
(2) the "revolutions" these companies have promised have always failed to
materialise. The body of the piece then historicises AI specifically as the
latest instance of that recurring pattern, with Herbert Simon (1965) and
Marvin Minsky (1970) AI predictions as the contemporary…

Internationals Network for Public Schools, New Tech Network, New Visions for
Public Schools). The study matched 15 "network" high schools in California
